text.title: Survey Types, Design Process, and Standard UX Metrics
text.summary: Survey typology, six-step survey design process, and the four standard UX measurement instruments (NPS, SUS, CSAT, SEQ).

list.item: Satisfaction — measures user happiness with a product or experience (CSAT, NPS).
Task-based — collects post-task feedback on ease and usability (SUS, SEQ).
Preference — compares options or prioritizes features (A vs B, ranking).
Exploratory — understands usage patterns and behaviors at scale.

rule.statement: Define a specific, measurable research objective before writing any question; never start with question ideas and work backward to an objective.
rule.rationale: Ill-formed objectives produce questions that collect data no one acts on; a clear objective constrains the question set and prevents survey bloat.

list.item: Step 1: Define objective — what specific, measurable thing do you want to know?
Step 2: Choose question types — multiple choice for fixed options, rating scale for intensity, ranking for prioritization, open-ended only for exploration.
Step 3: Write questions — one concept per question, neutral wording, complete answer options.
Step 4: Design flow — introduction + consent → screening → easy questions → core questions → demographics → thank you.
Step 5: Pilot test with 5-10 people; time the median completion; fix any confusion before launch.
Step 6: Analyze — calculate response rate, descriptive statistics on quantitative items, theme-code open-ended responses.

section.title: Standard UX Metrics

rule.statement: Use validated instruments verbatim — do not reword SUS, NPS, or UEQ items; any wording change invalidates benchmark comparability.
rule.rationale: Benchmark databases (SUS mean=68, NPS range −100 to +100) are tied to exact wording; paraphrasing breaks the comparison.

list.item: NPS: "How likely are you to recommend [X] to a friend or colleague?" (0-10). Score = % Promoters (9-10) minus % Detractors (0-6). Range: −100 to +100.
SUS: 10 standardized alternating positive/negative items, 1-5 scale. Score = (pos_sum + neg_sum) × 2.5. Average = 68; above 80 = excellent; below 50 = poor.
CSAT: "How satisfied are you with [X]?" (1-5 or 1-7). Score = (satisfied responses / total) × 100.
SEQ: "Overall, how easy or difficult was this task?" (1-7, very difficult to very easy). Use after each task in usability testing. Benchmark = 5.5.

section.reference: Deterministic NPS and SUS calculator from CSV
